title: Hidden
sentDescription: He locked himself in the cold because it was the last thing the station could still offer him: a little time. His experiments weren't merely research, and what happened wasn't just an accident. Something had been grown in his lab, a thing that couldn't be contained. The most terrible part is not that he died alone. It is that, before the end, he could hear it outside the chamber… and knew it was listening too.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They sit the way people usually sit when a bus runs late. Unknown to them, the city has stopped keeping its promises. By habit, they still know how to wait inside a working system. Bags on laps. Eyes searching in anticipation. Hope reduced to the waiting posture of public transport passengers.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: When life is ordinary, people enjoy the luxury of leaving through doors. Exiting a home through a window means the house has stopped obeying the normal rules of life. The adult helps the child escape first, but looks back because they must rush to leave their home forever. Home is still there, but it's not there. That's why abandoning home hurts.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Evacuating with a potted plant isn't practical, but that's why it matters. This person carries the plant simply because it's alive and fits in their arms. And it comes from their home. When nothing about your home can be saved, one small green plant becomes proof that you still care and can make a difference.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They're listening in awe, but not because of the data being presented. They're awed by the fact that a single person carried the weight of humanity's first impossible decision. Evelyn James didn't save the world. No one did. But she saw the end coming and chose to build a path through it before most people even dared to look at the truth.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The artist observed a wild turkey closely enough to preserve it as a detailed memory. Orbit records this art as an instructional communication about food, movement, and season. Early humans passed down survival skills by using cave paintings such as these to educate others, track animals, or map their environments.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They're looking intently at more than just a rocket. They're looking at the only Exit door left on the Earth. Every person present feels the same hope to somehow be on that rocket. If it launches successfully, life continues. If it fails, death. And the future shrinks that much smaller for humanity. That's why no one is celebrating yet. Hope this large isn't light. It has weight. It makes people stand still as they await the final verdict.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This image appears to organize space into paths, boundaries, and connected areas. Orbit stores it as an early example of a map. It details important areas that might contain sources of food. Humans survived by communicating geospatial data in a way that others could understand.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: No system is capable of properly cataloging this kind of tragic moment. It's not recorded as a policy failure nor as a launch failure. A child accidentally took one step in the wrong direction. Then, the child's guardians learn exactly how little time they have to save what they love.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Two bison face each other with enough care that the meeting itself becomes the memory. Orbit records this as sustained attention toward powerful animals, noting their weight, posture, danger, and importance. Early humans learned survival by watching other life closely, then they wrote that knowledge into stone so it wouldn't end with one hunter or one season.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A human hand was turned into a lasting mark. The dense internal pattern suggests the maker was not just showing a body part, but making the hand carry identity, memory, or claim. Orbit stores this as an early declaration of presence. The artist used stone to say that a living person had touched the world and wanted recognition for it.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They're not sightseeing. They're trying to gauge distance with their eyes. They're looking for a boat, a body, a sign, a reason, any indication that the ocean might give back something it had taken. Sometimes, people stand at a railing because they need to keep hoping and believing.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They're trying to make a disaster more understandable. They're using a whiteboard to plan out their actions. By reducing a disaster down to names, routes, rooms, numbers, arrows... fear can be turned into a task. Humans do this whenever situations become too large to survive emotionally.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They were trying to join the Exodus. They were supposed to be part of something that united humanity. Instead, what they truly witnessed was division. Who gets to ascend? Who had to stay behind? Who got to become part of the future, and who became forgotten memories? In a situation like this, a rocket is never just a vehicle. It's your final judgement made visible for everyone to see.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Human shapes carved into an enduring form. Orbit will archive this art as an instance of early self-recognition. The image may carry meanings related to ceremony, danger, teaching, or play. Yet, the central lesson remains clear. Artists portrayed their bodies in a form that could outlive them.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Large fish-like forms are placed beside a human body, making scale part of the memory. Orbit records this as knowledge of water, food, movement, and dependence. Whether these forms represent fish, vessels, or a larger water system, the lesson is that humans learned to survive by understanding life beneath the waters.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They're not praying to a god. They're essentially praying to the machine because the machine still provides heat and fuel, at least for one more day. When a pump becomes something you beg, the world has already ended. It merely hasn't ended for you just yet.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The healthy worker stayed at the side of the fallen one. That's the part that matters. No regulations required it, and it certainly didn't fit into their work schedule. One person couldn't continue, so the other remained to comfort. And all around them, the station's operations were neglected and fell apart.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: These people are waving to a passing boat. This is the moment rescue becomes visible, but still doesn't arrive. That may be worse than never seeing it at all. The people wave because they believe the boat hasn't noticed them. It has. But, they keep waving because accepting that truth would mean the end of hope.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This isn't ceremony. It's work that happens when ceremony isn't possible any longer. That corridor must be kept open. People still need to pass. So, two survivors carry the dead with whatever care they can still manage. All the while, life continues around a space that should've been allowed to stop and reflect.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The children are keeping the game going. They aren't ignoring the win conditions exactly. They're defeating them for a few seconds at a time. A bouncing ball makes the world feel alive. It promises endless motion: up, down, and back to the hand. Their laughter is small against this dying world. Yet, in that moment, they at least conquer their room.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This family lived trapped in a place meant to be temporary. Yet, their child kept growing anyway. So, they marked the growth milestones on the wall, because growth is a kind of defiance against despair. The world continued to narrow into a terrible place of waiting, shortages, and fear. Yet, the child still grew taller. Someone needed to notice that.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This is the moment a life stopped being a child and became a responsibility sealed away until it could be used. They placed the unborn into the machine because the world could no longer hold them warm.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: There's no correct way to divide an insufficient amount of food. The adult isn't solving the hunger problem, only choosing how best to avoid disappointing either child. She anguishes over the choice, but she still makes it with great care. Even love is forced to become arithmetic when survival is at stake because numbers never forgive.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They are looking at the coating like proof of success. White, clean, untouched by salt, water, rot, and even time. That was the promise of Oceanmark White. It helped the human world resist the ocean. Unfortunately, every painted surface survived too well. This coating didn't return to nature. It never broke down into more basic components. Surfaces only remained pristine because everything living in the surrounding biosphere paid the price.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This vent wasn't built for this purpose. It served as a means to move heat, gas, pressure, and waste. Yet, people found warmth there, and so they gathered close to it. The human body doesn't care how it survives, only that it does. Toxic methane infrastructure became a shelter, not because its purpose changed, but because humans had nowhere kinder left to go.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The shark obeyed the command to die. That's the unbearable part. It was born and built to guard, hunt, return, and submit. When the final command came, it dutifully swam itself to the termination platform like a loyal machine. Yet, it wasn't just a machine. The operator understood too late that they created a living machine capable of suffering and feeling fear. Still, they required it to die according to protocol.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Nobody is doing anything here. They're all waiting, and that's the point. The whole day has been reduced to the narrow hope that one machine will start working again. Every bucket in that line is a reminder that thirst never feels dramatic in the beginning. It's a growing presence that waits alongside you and never leaves.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Survivors never want to remember these moments. That raft can't hold three people. Allowing another person onboard means death for everyone. So, they must get the raft moving as fast as possible because neither survivor wishes to force the hands of the swimmer away. They don't look back either because looking would make the swimmer human again. The swimmer knows their decision too, but the body tries to survive anyway.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The smile isn't due to happiness. It's the last tool left when medicine, rescue, and power have all failed. Another face smiles in that room. It becomes the boy's last comfort.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The wheelchair slows down the evacuation effort, and that's exactly why this matters. Survival always rewards speed, strength, and the ability to maneuver. This person refuses that logic. Against all odds, they push, pull, lift, and keep the wheelchair steady.  When survival completely abandons the slow, vulnerable, and fragile, it has already lost an essential part of its purpose.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The Caretaker came back because that's what it was programmed to do. It didn't know the dock was empty, or that the hands meant to load new cargo were gone. This is the tragedy of useful machines. They complete their journeys in perfection, yet still return too late.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The boy isn't fishing because the fishing is good here. The world's biosphere is dying. He's fishing to give his waiting a purpose. As long as the line remains in the water, there's still a chance to catch some food, small as it may be. Pulling the line in empty means facing the reality of starvation.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A single blanket can't fix anything. It can't make food appear, restart power, or bring rescue closer. What it can do is hold the heat generated by two human bodies. When people have almost nothing left to give, they'll still give warmth.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This is what the end of the world looks like in its administrative phase. They weren't counting ordinary stock. They were counting how many chances humanity still had, and how few were left.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They were not animals in the old sense anymore, and not people in the protected sense either. That was the gap MOSES used. The subjects learned pattern, fear, memory, and song, but the system only measured response. By the time anyone understood what the songs meant, the cages had already become the answer.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A waiting line is a promise that someone knows the proper order of things. That's what makes this scene tragic and cruel. Waiting in line is what civilized people do when they believe a system still exists. But those doors will remain closed, so now the line slowly transforms into their only way to avoid panic.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This is the posture of a person standing beside a decision they can't undo. Behind that door were lives still waiting to begin. In front of the door stood someone forced to leave them there.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The helmet is too large. That means the lesson is too heavy. The adult is showing the child the machine because the machine has become part of their family's survival. It's something to understand, fear, and keep in operation at all costs.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They left the door open so the container would feel less like storage. Less like they had been packed away. One child has already crawled inside. One still stands at the threshold. The parent offers rations to them, as if trying to demonstrate that this arrangement can function, that a steel box can become a living space if people tried hard enough.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They gathered around the burner because it still gave off heat. It didn't provide comfort exactly. Just a little heat. Enough to make a circle. Enough for one person to rest, one to warm their hands, and one to keep watch in the darkness.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They knew the water could quietly take one away from the other. So, they built a promise out of cloth. If one began to disappear, the other would feel it. They intend to survive together or die together.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: On the drowned Earth, there are no solemn funeral processions. No decorated graves. No hallowed ground. Just two people bidding farewell. They're giving their dead to the very same water that took everything else.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Their hydroponics tray is quite small, but both people act like it contains their entire future. The person holding the light isn't trying make the plants more visible. They're using it as a makeshift grow light. They're desperately trying to ensure that food can still grow under human care. A tiny garden becomes enormous when all other gardens have failed.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The screen is dead, but the person keeps a hand there anyway. Humans do this when a connection is gone but their muscle memory still remembers where it used to be. Touch becomes the last message. Not sent, not received, but held in place because letting go makes the silence total and complete.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Safety lies above them, but reaching it still requires one final effort. It's hard to watch. Safety is visible, but not yet reached. Exhausted arms, slippery hands, wet rope, and crowded bodies all work against them. Even at the edge of survival, people still had to climb.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This is housing reduced to allocation. A number on a steel door becomes kitchen, bedroom, shelter, and future. The MOSES official only needs to point. The family has no choice but accept the decision.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They're not climbing toward safety. Not yet. They're climbing to gain a little more time. When water claims all the ground, going up becomes the only escape. One person holds the ladder stable against the rushing water because trust is a survival necessity. If those hands fail, any future above falls into the water with them.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Nothing dramatic is happening here. There's no running or shouting. Just three people giving part of their lives to a pipe that may or may not answer their plea for water. When a whole day can be spent waiting for one bucket of water, that's when you know survival defines human existence.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The adult is holding both the child and the flotsam because holding both makes them all more stable. The flotsam was never meant to save anyone. It just happened to remain above water when everything else didn't.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The rooftop isn't their home, but it's the last part of their home still above water. They rescued the dog because love isn't practical. Unfortunately, the rest of the world is coldly practical. So, this family waits together on top of the highest piece of what used to be theirs, trying to make the area comfortable as they wait for a rescue that never comes.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They're moving a hospital stretcher through a hallway because rooms no longer mean what they're supposed to mean... a secure place. The sick person is still being moved with care, and that matters. Even when systems and civilizations fail, people still keep trying to deliver each other to someplace safer.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: That person doesn't consider the seats to be empty. At least, not yet. They're being reserved and protected. They're being kept available for people who are permanently late. The bags say, "They're coming back." The person's posture says, "I know they're not."

title: Hidden
sentDescription:
The child is lifted above the crowd, above the water, above fear for as long as the adult can hold them.
It's a simple act of love; it's almost ordinary. That's why it hurts. Parents always lift their children to give them a higher perspective. They do it here so the child can see hope for a little longer.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Without radios or flares, the human body itself must communicate the message. They're spending their last strength in an effort to be seen by a distant passing vessel. They wave desperately, not because they believe they'll be seen, but because not waving means surrendering to an eventual death.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Cutting a child's hair does nothing to improve their chances of survival, but that's what makes the act meaningful. The parent still wanted the child to feel seen, clean, and still human. Even as the world collapses, this family made time to groom themselves. They didn't do it for their bodies, but for their souls, so that they wouldn't go feral.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The person speaks into the radio because hearing silence is worse than continuing to try. A radio gives hope a process: press, call, wait, and listen. The cruel part is that waiting feels like an action, too. For a little while, people can make themselves believe that an answer is only delayed for some reason, instead of gone forever.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Against the vastness of the ocean, a flag only appears as a tiny speck. However, the survivor waves it anyway because giving up is another kind of death. A companion watches from below, not because they know help is coming, but because hope sometimes needs a witness in order to keep trying.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: There isn't a safe way to do what they're doing. Rowboats are highly sensitive to shifting weight and can easily capsize if their center of gravity moves too much to one side. However, the man's inflatable raft is failing, so they have no choice but to attempt a dangerous transfer. The real risk isn't the handover of the child. It's when he attempts to climb aboard. We don't know the end result of their actions.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They're staring out through the exit of their home. The door sits open, but leaving forever still requires one of them to step forward and make it real. They hesitate because that first step will divide their life into a before and after. Their suitcases are ready. Their home is ready to be abandoned. Yet, the people are still trying to belong to it a few more moments.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They're not carrying ordinary cargo. They're carrying unborn lives with a care usually reserved for the dead or the divine. One advances carefully, while the others look back.  They know they're leaving behind far more than they can save.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A human form reduced to its simplest details: a head, a body, four limbs, and a stance. Orbit stores this as early proof that humans wanted to see themselves from an exterior viewpoint. Before cameras, mirrors, or archives, they recorded the shape of being human.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Humans placed themselves beside animals larger, faster, and stranger than themselves. Orbit reads this as observation and humility. Early humanity understood that it lived among other powerful beings, and that survival began by watching them carefully.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They're cooking over heat that should never have been there. The planet is leaking methane, the machine is failing to contain it, and so the ground gives off the wrong kind of warmth. Still, they decide to make a meal of it. This scene represents human survival at its smallest and strangest, when you can turn an aspect of global disaster into dinner. Hunger cannot wait for a better world.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A single figure that has been given structure, weight, and attention. Orbit records this as a possible image of a prominent role, someone marked as different, central, or wielding authority inside the group. Regardless if the image was portraying ceremony, warning, leadership, or transformation, the lesson is useful. Humans didn't just use art to only remember animals and places. They also defined social hierarchy.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A stag is shown calling out during mating season. Its head is raised. The antlers are extended. The bellow is implied. Orbit records this as humans learning about seasonal life. Animals weren't just resources. Their posture, timing, and behavior told humans when the world was changing, and how survival had to change with it.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The baby crosses first because the baby can't choose.  No safety exists here, just trust stretched across thin air. For one terrible second, the future is held by careful hands, not by systems, plans, or rescue orders.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Neither person is wrong enough to make the decision easy. The right direction could lead to rescue. The other direction might lead to shelter. Waiting could mean death. So does moving. Hope has split into two pointing hands. The person between them must choose which future to betray.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Keys symbolize an eventual return. Here, they mean the opposite. One person is giving away a home, a room, a duty, or a life they can no longer keep. There's no ceremony for this kind of ending. Just a small piece of metal pressed into another person's hand. The former owner turns away before they can feel anything.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: They know the cabinet isn't enough to barricade the door by itself, but enough isn't available. Instead, they used whatever weight, wood, and metal was lying around, augmented by their fear. The furniture must serve as a wall because the real walls have failed them. The door is still a door. The room is still a room. But, safety has become something people must physically hold in place.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Hands placed among animals is an act that joins the human body to the living world around it. Orbit records this as a memory of presence and relation. Humans saw these creatures, depended on them, feared them, and marked themselves beside them. The image teaches that early humanity understood itself through the other lives it shared the world with.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A wild boar that an artist chose to preserve. Orbit records this as a need to instruct others about the important details of the animal, such as its appearance, shape, weight, danger, and usefulness. Early humans respected the sources of their food. What could feed them could also wound them, and that made it valuable to remember.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A human hand sought to make their cave feel less empty. These marks may have carried meanings related to signs, paths, names, warnings, or memory. Orbit records the deeper lesson: humans used stone to store meaning that could live beyond the moment it was born.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Humans recorded animals, not as background but as part of the same world. Food, danger, weather, movement, and memory may all be present here. Orbit stores the image as evidence that early humans learned about the natural world by placing themselves inside the living patterns that they could perceive.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Many bodies. Many animals. Shared movement across stone. Orbit records this as a memory of group life: humans hunting, watching, following, gathering, and acting together. The image survives because the need to communicate this information outlived the people who made it.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Several animals occupy the same space, their bodies overlapping without losing their individual presence. Orbit records this as a layered work. The artist repeatedly returned to the same wall, or the same memory, and kept adding to it. The image teaches that early artwork didn't always depict a single event. Rather, it could be a place where many observations accumulated over time.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This image appears to gather several kinds of knowledge into one place: bodies, animals, marks, movement, and possible tools. Important information was placed here so it could be learned again.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This image records humans living from water, not only looking at it. The fish are shown with their insides known, suggesting food, anatomy, memory, and skill were connected. Orbit stores this as evidence that early humans learned survival by reading the bodies of other life, then preserved that knowledge for the group.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Humans placed themselves inside a field of powerful animals. Orbit reads this as a memory of scale, small bodies moving among larger, stronger beings. The image preserves more than the idea of prey. It shows that early humans understood danger as something to study, approach together, and turn into shared knowledge.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This image preserves animals in motion. Orbit reads it as a human attempt to portray a moment where life, food, fear, and skill intersected. They show humans needing to remember an intense experience that will likely be repeated in their lives.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This scene records humans in food-gathering activities. A figure climbs a tree, while hunters are grouped below. A task was not only remembered as actions, but as roles among people.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Many humans and animals move through the same recorded space. Orbit reads this as group coordination. The humans are collectively hunting together. The image teaches that human survival was rarely solitary. Knowledge wasn't useful unless everyone understood it and acted in unison.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: Bodies appear extended into lines, as if movement, rest, spirit, or memory has been reduced into a pattern. This image could be portraying a feeling of fear or something imagined beyond ordinary appearance.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This image shows thought becoming code. The marks no longer need to resemble a body or animal in order to communicate meaning. Orbit stores it as an early step toward shared symbols. Humans realized that a simple sign could hold memory, instruction, identity, or warning long after the speaker was gone.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: The upright figure and the animals share the same symbolic space. Orbit records this as evidence that humans imagined a connection between their bodies and power over animals. title: Hidden
sentDescription: Animals are placed inside a wider field of marks, suggesting that the creatures were remembered together with place, shelter, route, or event in mind. title: Hidden

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A painting of a horse created by a Stone Age artist who closely observed these animals in the wild. The extended lines might be symbolic wounds, an attempt at “hunting magic,” or a way to depict the animal's movement. Horses are one of the most frequently depicted animals in cave art, reflecting the deep emotional and psychological connections that prehistoric humans felt for them.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This image shows a mother mammoth and her calf. By showing the two animals moving forward together, the artist demonstrated an understanding of the kinship bond between them. Family relationships and the continuity of life across generations were recognized as meaningful.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This painting shows cave lions interacting with bison. The crosshatch markings could represent boundaries or hunter traps. Prehistoric humans used art to process their experiences and memories. This work reflects deep psychological connection with the natural world.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This image depicts an encounter between an aboriginal hunter and a kangaroo or wallaby. Orbit believes it to be instructional. The painting shows how to track the animal's movement and aim a barbed spear. The lesson isn't just about the hunt. Early humans understood the need to preserve and communicate their skills from one person to another.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: This image shows thought moving beyond bodies and animals into abstract patterns. Orbit stores it as evidence that humans learned to preserve relationships, paths, movement, or ideas without drawing the thing itself. The lesson is important. Abstraction allows memory to become lighter, stranger, and more portable.

title: Hidden

title: Hidden

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A horse remembered because of its speed. Orbit interprets this image as humans studying movement itself. The horse's legs are lifted. Its body is extended. title: Hidden
sentDescription: The image shows an outline sketch of a woolly mammoth, portrayed as enormous and powerful. In this case, a woolly mammoth represented a mountain of resources and therefore stood for a hunt that was both highly desirable and extremely dangerous.

title: Hidden
sentDescription: A bison carved into permanent record. Orbit archives this image as sustained focus upon this animal's strength, weight, and danger. The deeper lesson remains clear: early humans studied the lives of animals because preserving knowledge meant surviving another season more easily.

title: Hidden
